content.text: A composite SLA is the effective availability of an end-to-end system built from multiple Azure services. Individual services each carry their own SLA (e.g., 99.95% for App Service, 99.99% for Azure SQL). When you chain services together, the composite SLA depends on the topology: services in series multiply downtime probabilities (reducing composite availability); services in parallel multiply failure probabilities (increasing composite availability).

attrs.alt: Composite SLA: series topology (multiplicative, lower) vs parallel topology (higher)

content.text: In a series topology, a request must traverse every component — all components must be up for the request to succeed. The composite SLA is the product of all individual SLAs.

# Composite SLA for components in series
def series_sla(*slas: float) -> float:
    """
    Calculate the composite SLA for services arranged in series.
    All services must be available for the end-to-end request to succeed.
    
    Args:
        slas: SLA values as decimals (e.g., 0.9995 for 99.95%)
    Returns:
        Composite SLA as a decimal
    """
    result = 1.0
    for sla in slas:
        result *= sla
    return result

# Example: typical 3-tier web app
app_service_sla  = 0.9995   # 99.95% — Azure App Service
azure_sql_sla    = 0.9999   # 99.99% — Azure SQL Database
redis_cache_sla  = 0.9999   # 99.99% — Azure Cache for Redis

composite = series_sla(app_service_sla, azure_sql_sla, redis_cache_sla)
annual_downtime_hours = (1 - composite) * 8760

print(f"App Service:   {app_service_sla*100:.2f}%")
print(f"Azure SQL:     {azure_sql_sla*100:.2f}%")
print(f"Redis Cache:   {redis_cache_sla*100:.2f}%")
print(f"Composite SLA: {composite*100:.4f}%")
print(f"Annual downtime: {annual_downtime_hours*60:.1f} minutes ({annual_downtime_hours:.2f} hours)")

In a parallel (redundant) topology, a request can be served by any one of N instances. The system fails only when ALL instances fail simultaneously. The composite SLA is: 1 - P(all fail) = 1 - product of (1 - SLA_i) for each instance.

def parallel_sla(*slas: float) -> float:
    """
    Calculate the composite SLA for components in parallel (any one can serve).
    The system fails only if ALL components fail simultaneously.
    
    Args:
        slas: SLA values as decimals
    Returns:
        Composite SLA as a decimal
    """
    # P(all fail) = product of (1 - SLA_i) for each component
    p_all_fail = 1.0
    for sla in slas:
        p_all_fail *= (1 - sla)
    return 1 - p_all_fail

# Two App Service instances behind Azure Front Door
instance_a_sla = 0.9995
instance_b_sla = 0.9995

composite = parallel_sla(instance_a_sla, instance_b_sla)
annual_downtime_minutes = (1 - composite) * 8760 * 60

print(f"Instance A:    {instance_a_sla*100:.2f}%")
print(f"Instance B:    {instance_b_sla*100:.2f}%")
print(f"Composite SLA: {composite*100:.6f}%")
print(f"Annual downtime: {annual_downtime_minutes:.2f} minutes")

# Note: parallel does NOT mean "double the availability" — 
# 99.95% || 99.95% = 99.9975%, not 199.90%
Front Door (99.99%) + 2× App Service (99.95%) parallel + SQL DB HA (99.99%)
2× App Service regions (99.95%) parallel + SQL DB geo-rep (99.99%)
Standard tier or above; not Free/Shared
Single database; Business Critical adds in-region read replica
Read requests; 99.99% for RA-GRS read from secondary
Consumption plan; Dedicated/Premium plans same SLA
Premium tier; Standard tier has no SLA
Basic tier explicitly excluded from SLA; use Standard+
Basic tier services (Redis Basic, Service Bus Standard) explicitly carry no SLA. Never use Basic tier in production workloads where availability matters.
The following script calculates composite SLAs for arbitrary service chains, supporting both series and parallel components in a mixed topology.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Literal

@dataclass
class Component:
    name: str
    sla: float          # as decimal, e.g. 0.9995
    topology: Literal["series", "parallel"] = "series"

def calculate_composite_sla(components: list[Component]) -> dict:
    """
    Calculate composite SLA for a list of components.
    Components marked 'parallel' are evaluated as a parallel group first,
    then combined in series with the rest.
    """
    # Separate parallel groups from series components
    parallel_components = [c for c in components if c.topology == "parallel"]
    series_components   = [c for c in components if c.topology == "series"]

    # Compute parallel group availability (if any)
    if parallel_components:
        p_all_fail = 1.0
        for c in parallel_components:
            p_all_fail *= (1 - c.sla)
        parallel_group_sla = 1 - p_all_fail
    else:
        parallel_group_sla = None

    # Compose all series components + parallel group
    effective_slas = [c.sla for c in series_components]
    if parallel_group_sla is not None:
        effective_slas.append(parallel_group_sla)

    composite = 1.0
    for s in effective_slas:
        composite *= s

    annual_hours_down = (1 - composite) * 8760
    return {
        "composite_sla_pct": round(composite * 100, 6),
        "annual_downtime_minutes": round(annual_hours_down * 60, 2),
        "annual_downtime_hours": round(annual_hours_down, 4),
        "parallel_group_sla": round(parallel_group_sla * 100, 6) if parallel_group_sla else None,
    }


# Example: HA 3-tier app
# Front Door (series) → 2× App Service instances (parallel) → SQL DB (series)
architecture = [
    Component("Azure Front Door",  0.9999,  "series"),
    Component("App Service Inst A", 0.9995, "parallel"),
    Component("App Service Inst B", 0.9995, "parallel"),
    Component("Azure SQL DB",       0.9999, "series"),
]

result = calculate_composite_sla(architecture)
print(f"Composite SLA:         {result['composite_sla_pct']:.4f}%")
print(f"Annual downtime:       {result['annual_downtime_minutes']:.1f} minutes")
print(f"Parallel group SLA:    {result['parallel_group_sla']:.4f}%")

# Comparison: same arch without parallel instances (single App Service)
simple = [
    Component("Azure Front Door", 0.9999, "series"),
    Component("App Service",      0.9995, "series"),
    Component("Azure SQL DB",     0.9999, "series"),
]
simple_result = calculate_composite_sla(simple)
print(f"\nSimple architecture:   {simple_result['composite_sla_pct']:.4f}%")
print(f"Annual downtime:       {simple_result['annual_downtime_minutes']:.1f} minutes")
print(f"Improvement:           {simple_result['annual_downtime_minutes'] - result['annual_downtime_minutes']:.1f} minutes less downtime/year")

Microsoft Azure SLAs cover only the cloud infrastructure layer. The SLA does not apply to factors outside Microsoft's control. Understanding exclusions prevents incorrect assumptions about what the SLA covers.
SLAs exclude outages caused by customer code, misconfiguration, or exceeding resource quotas
SLAs exclude Azure Preview/Beta services — previews explicitly carry no SLA
SLAs exclude network problems between the customer's on-premises network and Azure
SLAs exclude Azure Free tier, Basic tier (Redis, Service Bus Standard), and Dev/Test subscriptions
SLAs are per-service, not per-region — Microsoft's obligation is to pay service credits, not restore data
Service credits under a breached SLA are typically 10%–25% of monthly charges for the affected period

A 99.9% SLA guarantees at most 8.76 hours of downtime per year from the provider's side. If your application also has its own failure modes (memory leaks, deployment errors, slow queries), your actual availability will be lower than the composite SLA calculation suggests.
Designing to a Target Composite SLA

Given a target SLA (e.g., 99.9%), you can work backwards to determine which components need redundancy or a higher service tier. The following script computes the minimum parallel instances needed to achieve a target composite SLA when one component is a bottleneck.

def instances_needed_for_target(
    single_instance_sla: float,
    target_composite_sla: float
) -> int:
    """
    Find the minimum number of parallel instances to achieve target_composite_sla.
    Each instance has single_instance_sla availability.
    
    Returns: minimum number of parallel instances required.
    """
    n = 1
    while True:
        # P(all n instances fail) = (1 - sla)^n
        p_all_fail = (1 - single_instance_sla) ** n
        composite = 1 - p_all_fail
        if composite >= target_composite_sla:
            return n
        n += 1
        if n > 10:  # practical upper bound
            return -1  # impossible with this SLA tier

# Example: App Service at 99.95% — how many instances for 99.999%?
single = 0.9995
targets = [0.999, 0.9999, 0.99999]
print(f"Single instance SLA: {single*100:.2f}%")
print(f"\n{'Target SLA':<12} {'Instances needed':<18} {'Achieved SLA'}")
print("-" * 50)
for t in targets:
    n = instances_needed_for_target(single, t)
    p_all_fail = (1 - single) ** n
    achieved = (1 - p_all_fail) * 100
    print(f"{t*100:.3f}%      {n:<18} {achieved:.6f}%")
Sensitivity Analysis: Effect of Each Component on Composite SLA

def sla_sensitivity_analysis(components: list[tuple[str, float]]) -> list[dict]:
    """
    For each component in a series chain, show how much composite SLA
    would improve if that component were upgraded to 99.999%.
    """
    # Base composite
    base = 1.0
    for _, sla in components:
        base *= sla

    results = []
    for idx, (name, original_sla) in enumerate(components):
        # Replace this component with 99.999%
        upgraded = [(n, 0.99999 if i == idx else s) for i, (n, s) in enumerate(components)]
        upgraded_composite = 1.0
        for _, sla in upgraded:
            upgraded_composite *= sla

        improvement_pct = (upgraded_composite - base) * 100
        improvement_min = improvement_pct / 100 * 8760 * 60  # minutes per year

        results.append({
            "component": name,
            "original_sla": original_sla * 100,
            "base_composite": base * 100,
            "upgraded_composite": upgraded_composite * 100,
            "improvement_pct_pts": improvement_pct,
            "improvement_minutes_year": improvement_min,
        })

    return sorted(results, key=lambda x: x["improvement_minutes_year"], reverse=True)


# 3-tier web application
chain = [
    ("Azure Front Door",  0.9999),
    ("App Service",       0.9995),
    ("Azure SQL DB",      0.9999),
    ("Azure Blob Storage", 0.9999),
]

results = sla_sensitivity_analysis(chain)
print(f"\nBase composite SLA: {results[0]['base_composite']:.4f}%")
print(f"\n{'Component':<25} {'Current SLA':<12} {'If upgraded to 5-nines':<25} {'Gain (min/year)'}")
print("-" * 80)
for r in results:
    print(
        f"{r['component']:<25} "
        f"{r['original_sla']:.2f}%       "
        f"{r['upgraded_composite']:.6f}%              "
        f"{r['improvement_minutes_year']:>8.1f}"
    )

Deploying services across Availability Zones (AZs) is the primary mechanism for improving individual service SLAs. AZ-redundant configurations keep the service available even if an entire datacenter fails. The SLA for zone-redundant services is typically 0.04–0.05 percentage points higher than single-zone deployments.

# Availability Zone improvement examples from Microsoft docs
zone_sla_comparisons = [
    {
        "service": "Azure Kubernetes Service",
        "without_zones": 0.995,   # 99.5%
        "with_zones":    0.9995,  # 99.95%
        "improvement_minutes_year": (0.995 - 0.9995) * -8760 * 60  # fix sign
    },
    {
        "service": "Azure Virtual Machines (single)",
        "without_zones": 0.9,     # 90% — single instance, no premium disk
        "with_zones":    0.9999,  # 99.99% — Availability Zone + premium SSD
        "improvement_minutes_year": None
    },
    {
        "service": "Azure SQL DB (General Purpose)",
        "without_zones": 0.9999,  # 99.99%
        "with_zones":    0.9999,  # 99.99% — already HA within region
        "improvement_minutes_year": 0
    },
    {
        "service": "Azure SQL DB (Business Critical)",
        "without_zones": 0.9999,  # 99.99%
        "with_zones":    0.9999,  # 99.99% — same SLA but zone-resilient
        "improvement_minutes_year": 0
    },
]

print(f"{'Service':<40} {'Without Zones':<15} {'With Zones':<12} {'Benefit'}")
print("-" * 85)
for s in zone_sla_comparisons:
    wout = s["without_zones"] * 100
    with_ = s["with_zones"] * 100
    base_downtime  = (1 - s["without_zones"]) * 8760 * 60
    zone_downtime  = (1 - s["with_zones"])    * 8760 * 60
    gain = base_downtime - zone_downtime
    benefit = f"{gain:.0f} min/yr less" if gain > 0 else "zone-resilient"
    print(f"{s['service']:<40} {wout:<15.2f} {with_:<12.2f} {benefit}")

A single Azure VM with Premium SSD but without an Availability Zone gets only a 90% SLA. Adding a second VM (Availability Set or AZ) raises it to 99.99% — a difference of nearly 9 hours vs 52 minutes of downtime per year.

Active-active multi-region deployments treat the two regions as a parallel group at the routing layer. Azure Front Door or Traffic Manager routes to either region; the system fails only if both regions are simultaneously down.

def multi_region_composite_sla(
    front_door_sla: float,
    region_a_composite: float,
    region_b_composite: float,
    database_sla: float,
    geo_replication: bool = True
) -> dict:
    """
    Calculate composite SLA for an active-active multi-region deployment.
    
    Architecture:
      Front Door (series) → [Region A || Region B] (parallel) → Database (series)
    
    If geo_replication=True, database SLA applies once (geo-rep handles failover).
    If geo_replication=False, database is a series dependency per region.
    """
    # Parallel group: both app regions
    p_both_fail = (1 - region_a_composite) * (1 - region_b_composite)
    app_tier_sla = 1 - p_both_fail

    # Series chain: Front Door → app tier → database
    composite = front_door_sla * app_tier_sla * database_sla

    annual_minutes_down = (1 - composite) * 8760 * 60

    return {
        "front_door_sla":       front_door_sla * 100,
        "region_a_sla":         region_a_composite * 100,
        "region_b_sla":         region_b_composite * 100,
        "app_tier_parallel_sla": app_tier_sla * 100,
        "database_sla":         database_sla * 100,
        "composite_sla":        composite * 100,
        "annual_downtime_minutes": annual_minutes_down,
    }


# Scenario: active-active across East US + West US
result = multi_region_composite_sla(
    front_door_sla=0.9999,        # Azure Front Door
    region_a_composite=0.9995,    # App Service East US
    region_b_composite=0.9995,    # App Service West US
    database_sla=0.9999,          # Azure Cosmos DB multi-region write
    geo_replication=True
)

print("Multi-Region Composite SLA Breakdown:")
for key, val in result.items():
    if isinstance(val, float) and key != "annual_downtime_minutes":
        print(f"  {key:<30}: {val:.6f}%")
    elif key == "annual_downtime_minutes":
        print(f"  {key:<30}: {val:.2f} minutes/year ({val/60:.2f} hours/year)")
Monitoring Actual SLA vs Contractual SLA

The composite SLA calculation is theoretical — based on Microsoft's contractual guarantees. Actual availability should be measured against the composite target using Azure Monitor availability metrics or synthetic transaction monitoring.

import requests
from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential

def get_app_service_availability_pct(
    subscription_id: str,
    resource_group: str,
    app_service_name: str,
    days: int = 30
) -> float:
    """
    Retrieve the actual availability percentage for an App Service
    over the past N days using Azure Monitor metrics API.
    """
    credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
    token = credential.get_token("https://management.azure.com/.default").token
    headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}

    resource_id = (
        f"/subscriptions/{subscription_id}/"
        f"resourceGroups/{resource_group}/"
        f"providers/Microsoft.Web/sites/{app_service_name}"
    )
    end_time   = datetime.now(timezone.utc)
    start_time = end_time - timedelta(days=days)

    url = (
        f"https://management.azure.com{resource_id}/"
        f"providers/microsoft.insights/metrics"
        f"?api-version=2023-10-01"
        f"&metricnames=Availability"
        f"&aggregation=Average"
        f"&interval=P1D"
        f"&timespan={start_time.strftime('%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ')}/"
        f"{end_time.strftime('%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ')}"
    )
    resp = requests.get(url, headers=headers)
    metrics = resp.json()

    values = []
    for metric in metrics.get("value", []):
        for ts in metric.get("timeseries", []):
            for point in ts.get("data", []):
                avg = point.get("average")
                if avg is not None:
                    values.append(avg)

    if not values:
        return 0.0

    avg_availability = sum(values) / len(values)
    return avg_availability


avail = get_app_service_availability_pct(
    "<subscription-id>",
    "production-rg",
    "contoso-web-prod",
    days=30
)
target_sla = 99.95
status = "MEETING SLA" if avail >= target_sla else "BELOW SLA"
print(f"Actual availability (30d): {avail:.4f}%")
print(f"Target SLA:                {target_sla:.2f}%")
print(f"Status: {status}")
print(f"Downtime in period: {(1 - avail/100) * 30 * 24 * 60:.1f} minutes")

Composite SLAs express end-to-end system availability as a function of individual service SLAs and their topology. In series: composite = product of all SLAs — always lower than the weakest component. In parallel: composite = 1 - P(all fail) — always higher than any single component. Real-world architectures mix both: front door (series) routes to redundant app instances (parallel) backed by a highly available database (series). Always check whether each service tier carries an SLA — Basic and Free tiers are explicitly excluded.
Series topology: multiply all SLAs — every hop reduces composite availability
Parallel topology: 1 - P(all fail) — redundancy lifts availability dramatically
99.9% ≠ 99.99%: the difference is 8.76h vs 52.6m downtime per year
Preview services, Free tier, and Basic tier services carry no SLA guarantee
Target composite SLA should inform redundancy decisions — adding a zone-redundant AKS cluster jumps availability from 99.5% to 99.95%
